risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "20 consecutive patients ... were included in the study and randomised to two groups (levosimendan and control groups)." Comment: The paper states that patients were "randomised" but does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer random number table, coin toss, blocked randomization method). Consecutive enrolment does not establish random sequence generation, so the method cannot be judged as truly random.

Summary: The paper states only that 20 consecutive patients were "randomised to two groups" and gives no description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, opaque sealed sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Randomization is described, but concealment is not, so allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The report states that patients were randomized to levosimendan vs control and that "The anaesthetic and surgical management of all patients was the same in both groups," but it does not say whether participants, clinicians, or other personnel were blinded, nor whether levosimendan was given in a placebo-controlled or masked fashion. Because the paper does not specify who knew the allocation, performance bias cannot be judged confidently.

Summary: The outcomes were measured by invasive haemodynamic monitoring using a thermodilution catheter and a computer system: "Measurements were performed using a 7F Multiflex thermodilution catheter..." and "PVR and SVR values were calculated and recorded by the computer system." These are objective physiological measurements, so even though the paper does not state that an outcome assessor was blinded, knowledge of allocation would be very unlikely to influence the outcome measurement. Comment: outcome assessment appears to be by a lab/imaging/system-style automated measurement rather than subjective judgement.

Summary: The study reports complete follow-up/analysis with no attrition: 'N lost to follow‐up or withdrawn: 0' and 'N analysed: 20' for 'N randomised:20'. The Results also do not describe any post-randomization exclusions or missing outcome data. Comment: With essentially complete follow-up and all randomized participants analysed, attrition is very unlikely to bias the results.
